schools.summary: Gill: Jerusalem signifies 'the possession of Salem,' its ancient name; the king Adonizedek, 'lord of righteousness'; he heard of Ai and Gibeon's peace.

themes: when Adonizedek king of Jerusalem had heard how Joshua had taken Ai... and... Gibeon had made peace
the report of God's work striking fear into his enemies
Melchizedek king of Salem, king of righteousness (Heb. 7:1)

schools.summary: Gill: the king and people feared lest they fall as Jericho and Ai; Gibeon was a great city, greater than Ai, its men mighty.

themes: they feared greatly, because Gibeon was a great city... and all the men thereof were mighty
the wicked trembling when the strong are won to God
the LORD shall cause thine enemies to be smitten before thee (Deut. 28:7)

schools.summary: Gill: Hebron, twenty-two miles from Jerusalem, an ancient city built seven years before Zoan in Egypt.
Clarke: the kings summoned together.

themes: Adonizedek king of Jerusalem sent unto Hoham king of Hebron... Piram... Japhia... and Debir
the confederacy of the wicked against God's people
they are confederate against thee (Ps. 83:5)

schools.summary: Gill: he thought himself no match for Gibeon, a great city with others subject to it, and judged Joshua would come to its aid; the cause was Gibeon's peace with Israel.

themes: Come up unto me, and help me, that we may smite Gibeon: for it hath made peace with Joshua
Gibeon assailed for joining God's people
all that will live godly shall suffer persecution (2 Tim. 3:12)

schools.summary: Gill: though Hittites and Jebusites dwelt among them, the Amorites were the most powerful and gave their name to all; they encamped before Gibeon and made war.

themes: the five kings of the Amorites... encamped before Gibeon, and made war against it
the whole power of the south arrayed against the submitted city
though an host encamp against me, I will be confident (Ps. 27:3)

schools.summary: Gill: some think they sent when besieged; it showed their faith in the power of God whom they now professed; they begged Joshua to come quickly and save them.

themes: Slack not thy hand from thy servants; come up to us quickly, and save us, and help us
casting themselves on the covenant in their need
he will ever be mindful of his covenant (Ps. 111:5)

schools.summary: Gill: Gilgal lay low in the plains of Jericho.
Keil & Delitzsch: Joshua advancing in answer to the petition with all the people of war.

themes: So Joshua ascended from Gilgal, he, and all the people of war with him, and all the mighty men of valour
honouring the league, marching to the rescue
a brother is born for adversity (Prov. 17:17)

schools.summary: Gill: Joshua likely consulted the Lord, having some doubt whether to engage, the war not being directly for Israel but for Gibeon; the LORD promised none should stand before him.

themes: Fear them not: for I have delivered them into thine hand; there shall not a man of them stand before thee
the victory granted before the battle
fear not, for I am with thee (Isa. 41:10)

schools.summary: Gill: the sudden coming threw them into consternation and confusion.
Keil & Delitzsch: Joshua coming suddenly, going up all night.

themes: Joshua therefore came unto them suddenly, and went up from Gilgal all night
faith in the promise joined with diligent action
be strong and work: for I am with you (Hag. 2:4)

schools.summary: Gill: the Lord disturbed, troubled, and frightened them at Israel's sudden approach.
Keil & Delitzsch: the great slaughter at Gibeon.

themes: the LORD discomfited them before Israel, and slew them with a great slaughter at Gibeon
the victory plainly the LORD's own work
the LORD shall fight for you, and ye shall hold your peace (Exod. 14:14)

schools.summary: Gill: the going down of Beth-horon, a narrow passage on the descent toward Azekah; more died by the hailstones than by the sword.

themes: the LORD cast down great stones from heaven upon them... more which died with hailstones than... with the sword
God turning the elements into weapons for his people
he gave them hail for rain, and flaming fire (Ps. 105:32)

schools.summary: Gill: Joshua spoke to the Lord in prayer, that the sun and moon might stand still until the victory was complete; he said, Sun, stand still upon Gibeon, and Moon, in Ajalon.

themes: Sun, stand thou still upon Gibeon; and thou, Moon, in the valley of Ajalon
the boldness of faith laying hold on God's power
if ye have faith... say unto this mountain, Remove (Matt. 17:20)

schools.summary: Gill: the sun, that comes out like a bridegroom and rejoices as a strong man, stopped at once, and the moon stayed; it hasted not to go down about a whole day.

themes: the sun stood still, and the moon stayed... about a whole day
the Creator halting the lights to serve his people
all continue according to thine ordinances: all are thy servants (Ps. 119:91)

schools.summary: Gill: this refers not to natural days but to a miraculous, extraordinary one, with no day like it before or after; the LORD fought for Israel.

themes: there was no day like that before it or after it, that the LORD hearkened unto the voice of a man
the astonishing power of believing prayer
the effectual fervent prayer of a righteous man availeth much (James 5:16)

schools.summary: Gill: he thought to have returned and prepared for it, but was prevented by hearing the five kings had hid in a cave at Makkedah; stated by anticipation.

themes: And Joshua returned, and all Israel with him, unto the camp to Gilgal
the return stated by anticipation, the work not yet done
he which hath begun a good work will finish it (Phil. 1:6)

schools.summary: Gill: not killed by hail or sword, but escaped, reserved by providence for a more shameful end.
Clarke: they hid themselves in a cave.

themes: these five kings fled, and hid themselves in a cave at Makkedah
God reserving the wicked for judgment
though they dig into hell, thence shall mine hand take them (Amos 9:2)

schools.summary: Gill: told him by his own people, or by inhabitants in his interest who had observed it.
Keil & Delitzsch: the report that the five kings were found.

themes: The five kings are found hid in a cave at Makkedah
the hiding of the wicked betrayed and discovered
there is nothing hid which shall not be manifested (Mark 4:22)

schools.summary: Gill: the stones were to keep the kings in until he had time to bring them before him and treat them as they deserved; men were set to keep the cave.

themes: Roll great stones upon the mouth of the cave, and set men by it for to keep them
God securing his enemies for their appointed reckoning
to reserve the unjust unto the day of judgment to be punished (2 Pet. 2:9)

schools.summary: Gill: not to stay at the cave but, having set a guard, pursue with all celerity.
Ellicott: the smiting of the hindmost (cf. Deut. 25:18).

themes: pursue after your enemies, and smite the hindmost... for the LORD your God hath delivered them
victories of God to be followed up to the full
we are more than conquerors through him that loved us (Rom. 8:37)

schools.summary: Gill: Joshua pitched at Makkedah while the rest of the army pursued the fleeing Canaanites.
Wesley: the children of Israel — the main body — made the slaughter.

themes: had made an end of slaying them with a very great slaughter, till they were consumed
God's judgment on his enemies thorough and near total
the LORD shall consume with the spirit of his mouth (2 Thess. 2:8)

schools.summary: Gill: they returned sound and well, not one killed or missing, 'sound and in full number.'
Keil & Delitzsch: all the people returning in peace.

themes: all the people returned to the camp to Joshua at Makkedah in peace: none moved his tongue
the perfect safety of God's people under his care
the LORD shall fight for you, and ye shall hold your peace (Exod. 14:14)

schools.summary: Gill: this was to roll away the great stones laid at the mouth of it; the kings were to be brought out to him.

themes: Open the mouth of the cave, and bring out those five kings unto me
God's reserved enemies brought forth for sentence
we must all appear before the judgment seat (2 Cor. 5:10)

schools.summary: Gill: they opened the cave by rolling away the stones.
Keil & Delitzsch: the five kings brought forth and named.

themes: brought forth those five kings... the king of Jerusalem... Hebron... Jarmuth... Lachish... and Eglon
the mighty of the land laid low as captives
he bringeth the princes to nothing (Isa. 40:23)

schools.summary: Gill: this was after sentence had been passed on them.
Clarke: the act was symbolic, a token not only of the present complete victory but of future triumphs.

themes: Come near, put your feet upon the necks of these kings... and put their feet upon the necks of them
the conquered enemies subdued under the feet of God's people
till I make thine enemies thy footstool (Ps. 110:1); Satan bruised under your feet (Rom. 16:20)

schools.summary: Gill: this respects future time and the other enemies they should meet, not the kings now helpless; thus the LORD would do to all their enemies.

themes: Fear not... be strong and of good courage: for thus shall the LORD do to all your enemies
past victory a pledge for future conflicts
be strong and of a good courage... he will not fail thee (Deut. 31:6)

schools.summary: Gill: Joshua smote and slew them with the sword, by his own hand or by others he ordered.
Clarke: they were slain and then hanged on five trees, the bodies exposed.

themes: Joshua smote them, and slew them, and hanged them on five trees... until the evening
the curse falling on God's enemies
Christ made a curse for us: cursed is every one that hangeth on a tree (Gal. 3:13)

schools.summary: Gill: the going down of the sun was the time fixed by God's law for taking down hanged bodies.
Wesley: taken down so neither beasts nor their people could come at them.

themes: at the time of the going down of the sun... they took them down off the trees, and cast them into the cave
God's judgment executed in order and by his law
let all things be done decently and in order (1 Cor. 14:40)

schools.summary: Gill: some say it was the day the sun stood still, but it seems the day the five kings were hanged.
Keil & Delitzsch: Makkedah taken the same day, none left remaining.

themes: that day Joshua took Makkedah, and smote it with the edge of the sword... he let none remain
the relentless progress of God's judgment
the iniquity of the Amorite now full (Gen. 15:16)

schools.summary: Gill: he passed on after taking Makkedah, destroying its inhabitants and king.
Keil & Delitzsch: him going with all Israel to Libnah.

themes: Then Joshua passed from Makkedah... unto Libnah, and fought against Libnah
the methodical advance of the conquest, city by city
they go from strength to strength (Ps. 84:7)

schools.summary: Gill: the Lord delivered it at once, no opposition being made that we read of.
Keil & Delitzsch: Libnah and its king delivered, smitten with the sword.

themes: the LORD delivered it also, and the king thereof, into the hand of Israel
every victory the gift of God's delivering hand
if God be for us, who can be against us? (Rom. 8:31)

schools.summary: Gill: Lachish was eight miles from Libnah, twenty from Jerusalem to the southwest.
Ellicott: Lachish has been variously identified.

themes: Joshua passed from Libnah... unto Lachish, and encamped against it, and fought against it
the methodical pressing-home of the victory
mighty through God to the pulling down of strong holds (2 Cor. 10:4)

schools.summary: Gill: the second day, either from the slaughter of the kings or of the siege, as Kimchi.
Keil & Delitzsch: Lachish delivered and smitten with the sword.

themes: the LORD delivered Lachish into the hand of Israel, which took it on the second day
God's deliverance sure though sometimes delayed
wait on the LORD... and he shall strengthen thine heart (Ps. 27:14)

schools.summary: Gill: Horam came up to help Lachish, perhaps the more induced because it had no king; Joshua smote him, leaving none remaining.

themes: Then Horam king of Gezer came up to help Lachish; and Joshua smote him... none remaining
those who aid God's enemies share their ruin
be not partaker of other men's sins (1 Tim. 5:22)

schools.summary: Gill: Eglon was eight miles from Lachish, twelve from Jerusalem southward.
Keil & Delitzsch: Joshua passing to Eglon with all Israel.

themes: from Lachish Joshua passed unto Eglon... and fought against it
the relentless, thorough march of the conquest
I press toward the mark (Phil. 3:13-14)

schools.summary: Gill: they took it the same day they encamped, the besieged unable to hold it.
Keil & Delitzsch: Eglon taken that day, smitten with the sword.

themes: they took it on that day... and all the souls that were therein he utterly destroyed that day
God hastening the victory once the enemy is broken
he will finish the work, and cut it short in righteousness (Rom. 9:28)

schools.summary: Gill: Hebron lay in the hill country, so they went up to it from lower Eglon, sixteen miles distant.
Clarke: the advance to Hebron.

themes: Joshua went up from Eglon... unto Hebron; and they fought against it
the conquest reaching even the heights
every high thing that exalteth itself against the knowledge of God (2 Cor. 10:5)

schools.summary: Gill: they took it and smote it with the sword.
Keil & Delitzsch: the king, all the cities, and all the souls smitten, none left remaining.

themes: they took it, and smote it with the edge of the sword... he left none remaining
God's judgment on the doomed entire
the destruction of the transgressors and sinners together (Isa. 1:28)

schools.summary: Gill: Debir, which he passed when going to Hebron and did not then fight, he returned and took, about a mile from Hebron.

themes: And Joshua returned... to Debir; and fought against it
leaving no stronghold of the enemy standing
cleanse your hands... and purify your hearts (James 4:8)

schools.summary: Gill: Debir was also a royal city with others dependent on it, five or six miles from Hebron; as he did to Hebron, so to Debir.

themes: he took it, and the king thereof... he left none remaining: as he had done to Hebron, so he did to Debir
the same righteous judgment dealt city by city
he will render to every man according to his deeds (Rom. 2:6)

schools.summary: Gill: the part of Canaan southward, hills and vales, well watered with springs; he destroyed all that breathed, as the LORD commanded.

themes: Joshua smote all the country... and all their kings... as the LORD God of Israel commanded
the conquest God's righteous judgment by his command
of the cities... thou shalt save alive nothing that breatheth (Deut. 20:16-17)

schools.summary: Gill: Kadesh-barnea lay to the south of Canaan.
Keil & Delitzsch: the smiting from Kadesh-barnea to Gaza, all Goshen to Gibeon.

themes: Joshua smote them from Kadeshbarnea even unto Gaza, and all the country of Goshen, even unto Gibeon
the wide sweep of the conquest, the promise made good
every place the sole of your foot treads I have given (Josh. 1:3)

schools.summary: Gill: 'at one time' means not one day but a very short time, a few days, as the history shows.
Clarke: he defeated all those kings and took their land at one time.

themes: all these kings and their land did Joshua take at one time, because the LORD God of Israel fought for Israel
every success owing to God fighting for his people
if God be for us, who can be against us? (Rom. 8:31)

schools.summary: Gill: he returned after all the kings and cities had been taken, the same stated by anticipation earlier.
Keil & Delitzsch: Joshua returning with all Israel to Gilgal.

themes: And Joshua returned, and all Israel with him, unto the camp to Gilgal
the army back at the place of the covenant
they go from strength to strength... before God (Ps. 84:7)
